turns.content: I'm getting a compilation error when trying to parse user input into my custom User struct. The error says I need to implement something from the std::str trait. What went wrong?

The error you're seeing is because your User type doesn't implement FromStr yet. You need to derive this trait so that str::parse::<User>() works on string inputs. If you derive it, the compiler will check if your fields match the expected format and return a ParseError if they don't.
Okay, I just derived FromStr but now I'm getting an error about missing fields in my struct definition. What should those fields look like?

Each field in your User struct needs to have an identifier that matches what the parser expects. For example if you want to parse a name and age string, your struct should define String for name and u8 for age. The derive macro will then generate code to extract those specific fields from the input string.
Wait, I thought I could just use any type. Can't I make a custom parser instead? Does FromStr force me to follow strict rules?

You can implement FromStr manually with a custom parser, but the derive approach is much more convenient for most cases. If you choose manual implementation, you'll need to handle error types yourself and return a ParseError when parsing fails instead of letting the compiler generate the logic.
I tried implementing it myself but now I'm getting an error about missing import statements. What exactly do I need to bring into scope?

You need to import the FromStr trait and the ParseError type from std::str. Without these imports, your manual implementation won't compile because the compiler can't find those types in your module namespace.
Got it, I added the imports but now my code isn't even running. The error says I'm missing some crates or dependencies. Is this a standard library issue?

This is actually just a standard library trait implementation. You don't need any external crates for basic string parsing. Just ensure your module has proper visibility so the compiler can see your custom types when checking against the FromStr requirements.
I think I finally got it working but now I'm wondering about error handling. If someone enters invalid data, what happens instead of returning an error?

When you call str::parse::<User>() on invalid input, it returns a Result type containing either the parsed User or a ParseError. You should check this result and handle the error case explicitly rather than letting it propagate silently.
